coredata.dc:title: Scotch-tape-like exfoliation effect of graphene quantum dots for efficient preparation of graphene nanosheets in water
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Quanzhu
Xia, Guangsen
Du, Min
Lu, Yonghong
Xu, Haibo
coredata.dc:description: As a simple and eco-friendly strategy, the production of graphene nanosheets (GNs) by liquid-phase exfoliation (LPE) faces a challenge to the improvement of exfoliation yield for scalability, as well as the stable dispersibility for application. Herein, we electrochemically prepare a kind of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with undestroyed crystalline structure on basal plane and a number of hydrophilic functional groups at edge, to exfoliate and disperse GNs in water with a high yield of 89–100%. The obtained GNs show a conductivity of 1.2 × 105 S m−1 and water dispersibility of 1.0 mg mL−1 for over one month, due to the firm adsorption of amphiphilic GQDs, which have non-covalent π-π bond interaction with graphene basal plane and strong H-bonds with water. The lateral size, functional groups and defect nature of GQDs have great influence on GNs and a scotch-tape-like exfoliation mechanism is proposed. GQDs as green stabilizer and exfoliation agent can effectively acquire the high-quality GNs and promote their potential application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Liquid-phase exfoliation
Graphene
High yield
High conductivity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and physical properties of GNs
Effect of material proportion and sonication time
Effect of size, functional group and defect of GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of 1-GQDs. (a) FL spectra (inset, blue FL at 365 nm excitation). (b) AFM. (c) Diameter distribution from DLS. (d) TEM. (e) HR-TEM and (f) XPS.
(a) FE-SEM. (b) HR-TEM and (c) AFM of GNs. (d) XPS and (e) Raman of graphite and GNs. (f) Relationship between the conductivity of graphene paper and washing cycle.
HR-TEM image of (a) 2-GQDs and (b) 3-GQDs. (c) FL spectra of 1-GQDs with different pH value excited at 365 nm. (d) Exfoliation yield by different GQDs. (e) AFM and (f) HR-TEM images of 6-GQDs.
Characterization of 7-GQDs. (a) AFM. (b) Height distribution from AFM. (c) Lateral size and (d) basal plane defects by HR-TEM.

head.ce:title: Scotch-tape-like exfoliation effect of graphene quantum dots for efficient preparation of graphene nanosheets in water

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering, China University of Petroleum (East China), Qingdao 266580, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingdao Haida Haixi New Materials Co., Ltd, Qingdao 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingdao Haida Haixi New Materials Co., Ltd

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, Qingdao 266100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a simple and eco-friendly strategy, the production of graphene nanosheets (GNs) by liquid-phase exfoliation (LPE) faces a challenge to the improvement of exfoliation yield for scalability, as well as the stable dispersibility for application. Herein, we electrochemically prepare a kind of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with undestroyed crystalline structure on basal plane and a number of hydrophilic functional groups at edge, to exfoliate and disperse GNs in water with a high yield of 89–100%. The obtained GNs show a conductivity of 1.2 × 105 S m−1 and water dispersibility of 1.0 mg mL−1 for over one month, due to the firm adsorption of amphiphilic GQDs, which have non-covalent π-π bond interaction with graphene basal plane and strong H-bonds with water. The lateral size, functional groups and defect nature of GQDs have great influence on GNs and a scotch-tape-like exfoliation mechanism is proposed. GQDs as green stabilizer and exfoliation agent can effectively acquire the high-quality GNs and promote their potential application.

Analogous to the microcleavage exfoliation of highly crystalline mono-layer graphene known as “scotch-tape” method by Geim, this liquid-phase exfoliation process can be visually depicted as a “scotch-tape-like exfoliation” by GQDs in water.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: The extraordinary physical properties of graphene, such as electronic, thermal, optical, and mechanical properties, have attracted much attention since its discovery [1,2], and numerous technology applications of graphene, e.g. in energy storage, optoelectronics conversion, as well as in detector and biological labeling, have been explored [3–5], and a number of production methods follow [6,7], such as micromechanical cleavage, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), chemical or electrochemical oxidation et al. [8,9]. For all, not only the requirements of low cost, environment-friendliness and scalability, but a demand of high-quality graphene nanosheets (GNs) (low defect, few layers and large size) are important [9,10]. Of course, these criteria are harsh and often self-contradictory, such as chemical oxidation method, whose high yield of GNs is always accompanied by rich defects and unavoidable damage to environment due to strong oxidants.

With the aid of mechanical force (e.g. sonication and shear) to overcome the interlayer stacking of graphite, liquid-phase exfoliation (LPE) provides a simple and eco-friendly route to preserve the crystalline structure of pristine GNs to a large extent. However, whose graphene yield is usually low due to the unmatched surface energy between GNs and the media to give easy restacking [11,12]. Organic reagents such as N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) [13,14] were used to disperse the GNs well, but a yield of 10–20% is not enough, and whose toxicity preliminarily impedes the usage. Alternatively, a higher degree of exfoliation combined with dispersion was realized by amphiphilic surfactants such as sodium dodecylbenzenesulfonate (SDBS) and sodium deoxycholate (SDC) [15,16] whose hydrophobic basal plane promotes their adsorption onto graphite which is beneficial to exfoliation, while the ionic hydrophilic heads give electrostatic repulsion to facilitate the dispersion. However, a high dispersant dose and the difficulty in separation retard the application, not to mention the degradation to intrinsic physical properties of GNs. Therefore, the suitable stabilizer and exfoliation agents are urgently needed.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nano-scaled graphene fragments (<100 nm) with many functional groups at edge (similar to graphene oxide (GO) and having same carbon structure as graphene) [17,18]. Besides the virtue of no toxicity, the amphiphilicity of GQDs seems attractive, and recently they are tried to help the dispersion or exfoliation of GNs by LPE in aqueous medium [19–21]. He et al. [19] added GQDs to stabilize 0.4 mg mL−1 graphene dispersion for >3 months. Zeng et al. [20] applied S-GQDs to exfoliate ~0.8 mg mL−1 graphene with a low yield (<20%). Now the exfoliation yield by GO has reached 40% [21]. Apparently, this unexpected result suggests that the characteristics of GQDs or GO have great impact on stability and exfoliation effect of GNs, and their influence factors and action mechanism are expected to be thoroughly studied to give further improvement.

In this work, with the aid of electrochemically oxidized GQDs with undestroyed crystalline structure on basal plane and many hydrophilic functional groups at edge, the high-quality and water-dispersable GNs are prepared with a yield of up to 89–100%. Furthermore, a scotch-tape-like exfoliation mechanism is proposed to clarify the function of GQDs as green stabilizer and exfoliation agent vividly.

Polyacrylonitrile-based carbon fiber (PAN-CF) came from Toray T700SC of Japan (12 K, ~7.0 μm fiber diameter). High expanded graphite was obtained from Qingdao Huatai Graphite Co., Ltd. (Shandong, China). Ammonia water (NH3H2O, 25%, AR), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%, AR), manganese dioxide (MnO2, AR), quinine sulfate ((CH24NO) H2SO2HO, BR), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, AR), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, AR) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, AR) were purchased from Sinopharm group. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Deionized water (resistivity: ~18.2 MΩ cm) obtained through a Milli-Q system was used throughout all experiments.

Seven GQDs were prepared as shown in Fig. S1 in Supporting information (SI). Under a constant voltage of 30 V, the electrochemical exfoliation was performed in a two-electrode cell, PAN-CF bundles as anode and Ti mesh (50 mm × 50 mm, TA2) as cathode in 6.0 mol L−1 NH3H2O electrolyte. 12 h later, 5.50 g PAN-CF was peeled off and the electrolyte was filtered through 0.1 μm millipore membrane (nylon membrane, Whatman). The filtrate was treated by thermal decomposition at 373 K to remove NH3. The resulting dark brown dispersion was denoted as 1-GQDs.

1-GQDs were dialyzed through a 2000 Da dialysis bag (Union Carbide Corporation) for 5 days. The dialysis solution outside the bag was collected and denoted as 2-GQDs (smaller size), while the inside was 3-GQDs (larger size). The mass ratio of 2-GQDs to 3-GQDs was 8:2 after vacuum drying.

After that, 100 mL 1.0 mol L−1 H2SO4, Na2SO4 and NaOH solution were added into 100 mL 1.0 mg mL−1 1-GQDs, respectively, and the dispersions were denoted as 4-GQDs, 5-GQDs and 6-GQDs. 1 h later, adding 1.0 mol L−1 NaOH solution into 4-GQDs, or 1.0 mol L−1 H2SO4 solution into 6-GQDs to adjust the pH value to 6–7, the fluorescence (FL) signals were detected before or after neutralization.

In contrast, 5.50 g PAN-CF bundles were chemically oxidized in H2O2 solution (300 mL, 10 wt%) with tip-sonication (400 W, 20 kHz) for 10 h at 373 K, and then adding excessive MnO2 to remove H2O2. The resulting dispersion was filtered through 0.1 μm millipore membrane to remove the insoluble residual, and the golden brown solution was denoted as 7-GQDs.

GNs were prepared in two steps: sonication-assisted exfoliation of graphite in 1-GQDs dispersion and the removal of 1-GQDs. Typically, 100 mg expanded graphite was added into 300 mL 1-GQDs dispersion with different concentration (0.07–0.7 mg mL−1), followed by tip-sonication (400 W, 20 kHz) for 30–120 min at room temperature. And then, the dispersion was filtrated through a 0.1 μm millipore membrane. To remove the 1-GQDs, the filtration residue was re-dispersed with deionized water, and the filtration and re-dispersing procedures were repeated several times until no signal can be detected in slurry by FL. The total mass of removed 1-GQDs came from the collected filtrate by vacuum drying at 373 K.

Next, the after-washing filtration residue was re-dispersed with deionized water via bath sonication for 10 min. Subsequently, the aqueous dispersion was centrifuged at 3000 rpm (relative centrifugal force: 805 g) for 5 min. The mass of GNs was obtained from supernatant by vacuum drying at 373 K, and the exfoliation yield was calculated as Eq. (1) in SI.

The stability of graphene dispersion was conducted by re-dispersing GNs into deionized water via bath-sonication for 10 min, and the graphene dispersions with different concentration were preserved for observation.

Graphene paper (thickness of 6.0 μm) supported on the polyethylene terephthalate (PET, thickness of 15.0 μm) substrate, was prepared by drop-casting deposition technique. Typically, the 1.0 mg mL−1 graphene dispersion was dripped on PET, and dried overnight at 333 K to remove the remaining water. Subsequently, it was rolled at 5.0 MPa and 353 K to obtain a PET-based graphene paper.

Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images were recorded using a Hitachi S4800 with an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained on a FEI TECNAI G2 F20 transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The test sample was obtained by drop-casting the aqueous dispersion on a carbon-coated copper grid, followed by drying at room temperature. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) images were obtained on a Digital Instrument Nanoscope (Veeco Multimode) atomic force microscope. The sample was prepared by drop-casting the dispersion on a clean mica wafer and dried at room temperature. A Malvern Zetasizer Nano Zs was used to measure the size by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential (ζ). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were carried out on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) with an Al Kα as the X-ray source. The sample was prepared by drop-casting the dispersions on a clean silicon wafer and dried at room temperature. Binding energy calibration was based on C1s at 284.6 eV. X-ray diffraction (XRD) of PAN-CF and GQDs were conducted by a Powder X-Ray Diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) that was operated at 40 kV and 40 mA. The FL spectra of the sample were recorded using a Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured using a Nicolet iS10 spectrometer. The Raman spectrum was measured using a Laser Confocal Micro Raman Spectroscopy (Labram Hr800, Horiba Jobin Yvon). The 532 nm radiation from a YAG laser was used as the excitation source. The electrical resistances were measured by a four-probe (RTS-8, Guangzhou Four Point Probe Instrument Co., Ltd.).

In this study, seven GQDs were fabricated by electrochemical or chemical method, as shown in Fig. S1 in SI. Fluorescence (FL) property is used as a finger print of GQDs [22], and a typical excitation-dependent FL behavior is shown in Fig. 1a, a confirmation of successful preparation of 1-GQDs. For 1-GQDs, atomic force microscopy (AFM) images (Fig. 1b) reveal a thickness of 0.4–1.5 nm while 85% of all is <1.0 nm (1–2 layers) (Fig. S2 in SI) [23]. A diameter distribution of 6.5–21.0 nm (an average of 11.1 nm) from (dynamic light scattering) DLS measurement is exhibited (Fig. 1c). The crystalline morphology by high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) shows a group of parallel crystal planes without being destroyed (Fig. 1d and e).

X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) shows C 1s (61.90%), O 1s (24.13%) and N 1s (13.97%) peaks (Fig. 1f). A high (O + N)/C ratio of 2:3 gives 1-GQDs a remarkably stable concentration of up to 6.0 mg mL−1, and an undestroyed crystalline structure of 1-GQDs with a number of functional groups at edge is indicated. From the C1s and N1s spectra fitted (Fig. S3 in SI), 1-GQDs contain oxygen and nitrogen functional groups including CN (286.2 eV), CO and/or O-C-N (287.9 eV), O-C=O and/or N-C=O (288.6 eV), pyridinic-N (398.6 eV), amine-N (399.4 eV), pyrrolic-N (400.1 eV) and graphitic-N (400.9 eV) [24,25]. Fig. S4 shows the typical XRD profiles for PAN-CF and 1-GQDs. The 1-GQDs have a broader (002) peak centered at ~25.110 degrees, and the interlayer spacing is 0.354 nm, which is bigger than that of the original PAN-CF (0.338 nm). This result could be attributed to the oxygen and nitrogen functional groups introduced in the oxidation and intercalation of PAN-CF, which increased the interlayer distance between graphene sheets. Normally, these oxide functional groups are attributed to the break of CC bonds and their subsequent oxidization by oxidant from water electrolysis [26], while the nitrogen-containing groups come from the residual NH4 +, and the inherent nitrogen of polyacrylonitrile-based carbon fiber (PAN-CF), based on our previous research [27]. Parviz et al. [28] concluded that the groups with great electronegativity would stabilize the positive charge well and enhance the interaction between the host and graphene layers. Besides strong electronegativity, oxygen and nitrogen atoms with lone-pair electrons and small atomic radius, easily have a tendency of forming strong electrostatic attraction with water by H-bonds. The strong H-bond between water and oxygen-functional groups of GO has been proposed [29], and the good dispersion behavior of GQDs was attributed to the solvent polarity and H-bonds.

The relative quantum yield of 1-GQDs reaches 15.61% (Table S1 in SI) in our laboratory, and about 20 g product in each batch is obtained (Fig. S5 in SI), which can easily be scaled-up by increasing the amount of raw material or volume of electrolyte cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and physical properties of GNs
ce:section.ce:para: The exfoliated GNs are characterized subsequently. Contrasted with the field-emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) image of worm-like expanded graphite with a lateral size of several hundred micrometers (Fig. S6 in SI), that of GNs clearly shows the transparent, smooth and thin flakes with crumpled silk veil (Fig. 2a), a typical morphology of few-layer graphene [30]. HR-TEM image shows that most of flakes are nearly 1.0–8.0 μm in size and with folds on edge. Randomly observing the folded edge of the flakes, it consists of 4–5 atomic layers with ordered graphite lattices (Fig. 2b). The distance between the ordered lattices is 0.34 nm, corresponding to the interlayer distance in graphite [31]. AFM image (Fig. 2c and Fig. S7 in SI) confirms a thickness of 0.5–2.0 nm (1.24 nm in average), and >80% of which are <5 layers.

XPS of expanded graphite shows two main characteristic peaks of C and O (C: 97.47%, O: 2.53%) (Fig. 2d). However, the GNs have three peaks of C1s, O1s and N1s (C: 89.23%, O: 7.58%, N: 3.19%). From the N1s spectrum (Fig. S8 in SI), the matching lines of GNs are consistent with those of 1-GQDs, and which is the only nitrogen origin, indicative of a firm adhesion of 1-GQDs on graphene surface to form graphene/GQDs nanocomposites, even after 6-time washing to no FL signal. The loading content is 150 mg 1-GQDs on 1.0 g GNs (a mass ratio of ~1:7) according to Eq. (1) and Fig. S9 in SI.

Raman spectroscopy has historically played an important role in structural characterization of graphitic materials [32,33]. It can be seen from Fig. 2e that the 2D peak of GNs (2685 cm−1) shows 15 cm−1 red shift and a higher intensity compared to that of graphite (2700 cm−1), which is an important feature for the conversion of graphite to graphene [34]. Moreover, Eckmann et al. [35] regarded the intensity ratio of ID/ID′ ≈ 7 as basal plane vacancy defects and ≈ 3.5 as edge or boundary defects. For our products, ID/IG is low to 0.128 (ID/IG ≈ 0.1 for graphite), and ID/ID′ is 1.154, far below the benchmark of edge defects, demonstrating that the prepared GNs remain the original basal plane structure of graphite, accompanied by some defects at edge. Similarly, high-energy mechanical force favors through-plane fracture rather than in-plane delamination, thus leading to the formation of edge defects [11].

To test the electrical performance of GNs, the conductivity change of polyethylene terephthalate (PET)-based graphene paper (photo in Fig. S10 in SI, 1.1 mg cm−2 graphene loading, ~6.0 μm thickness of GNs from FE-SEM in Fig. S11 in SI) with washing cycle is recorded (Fig. 2f). The conductivity gradually increases with the continuous removal of 1-GQDs and after 6 cycles, a maximum value of 1.2 × 105 S m−1 is obtained, much higher than others from LPE (Table S2 in SI) and indicative of the structural integrity of as-prepared GNs. The result also suggests that the firm adsorption of 1-GQDs on graphene surface keeps the intrinsic electrical property of GNs, but the randomly dispersed 1-GQDs does not. Tung et al. also thought that the GO could protect graphene from damage, favoring the formation of large flakes and high-quality graphene [21].

The stability of graphene dispersion is also investigated. The re-dispersion of GNs in water achieves a concentration of 1.0 mg ml−1 without any additives, and no precipitation was observed after one month. The high zeta potential (ζ) of −61.90 mV also proves its excellent colloidal stability [36]. Apparently, the graphene dispersion is endowed a distinctive water stability by 1-GQDs and which will absolutely widen its solution-processable application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of material proportion and sonication time
ce:section.ce:para: Moreover, the influences of addition proportion of 1-GQDs and the sonication time on exfoliation yield are investigated. Keeping the sonication time at 90 min, the yield rapidly increases from 52.5% to 89.0% with the increase of m1-GQDs/mgraphite (the mass ratio of 1-GQDs to graphite) from 1:5 to 1:1, and then almost keeps constant from 1:1 to 2:1 (Fig. S12 in SI). Considering the firm adsorption of ~1:7 mass ratio of 1-GQDs to GNs mentioned above, the results indicate that the excessive 1-GQDs largely improve the yield of GNs and they should be washed away after exfoliation to maintain the intrinsic electrical property of GNs. The excess of 1-GQDs probably increases their adsorption opportunity on graphite surface, and the exfoliation rate is observed to obviously decrease with the continuous adsorption of 1-GQDs and peeling-off of GNs in experiment. Of course, they can be recovered for reuse.

Subsequently, keeping m1-GQDs/mgraphite at 1:1, the exfoliation yield gradually increases from 80.2% to 88.3% with the increase of sonication time from 30 min to 75 min, and then hardly changes any more till 105 min (Fig. S13 in SI). So the sonication time is controlled for 90 min afterwards, shorter than others from LPE (Table S3 in SI). In addition to the reduced graphene flake size, long sonication of graphite was also proved to produce graphene atomic- or point-defects at edge [11]. Apparently, the influence of sonication time on exfoliation yield is not as remarkable as 1-GQDs proportion, which naturally reminds us of considering the surface characteristics of 1-GQDs, such as lateral size, functional groups and defect nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of size, functional group and defect of GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Lateral size of 2-GQDs and 3-GQDs from grading dialyses of 1-GQDs, is 3.0–12.0 nm (average size: 7.4 nm) for the former and 13.0–21.0 nm (average size: 17.7 nm) for the latter in HR-TEM images (Fig. 3a and b), and their ζ is −19.4 mV and − 25.4 mV, respectively. Both are used to exfoliate the expanded graphite in water with the m1-GQDs/mgraphite of 1:1 and sonication time of 90 min, acquiring a yield of 78.0% for 2-GQDs and 83.1% for 3-GQDs. The order of yield is 1-GQDs > 3-GQDs > 2-GQDs, which means the advantage of large GQDs over small ones and a possible existence of suitable size ratio for higher yield. The surfactants (e.g. SDC) with flat molecular structure also tend to adsorb strongly on graphite surface to have larger contact area per surfactant molecule compared to linear chain surfactants (e.g. SDBS) [16]. Thereby, the influence of mass ratios of 2-GQDs to 3-GQDs on yield is studied (Fig. S14 in SI) and a ratio of 3:7 achieves a yield of ~100%, indicative of a combined effect from both.

Besides, the colloidal stability of GQDs at different pH value is studied in terms of ζ [37]. The ζ is −33.50 mV for 1-GQDs (pH 7.28), −4.78 mV for 4-GQDs (by H2SO4 treatment, pH 0.25), −30.50 mV for 5-GQDs (by Na2SO4 treatment, pH 7.65) and −10.32 mV for 6-GQDs (by NaOH treatment, pH 12.92), respectively. Obviously, the colloidal stability of 1-GQDs is somewhat deteriorated after strong acid or alkaline treatment. 1-GQDs bear negative charges because of the large quantity of oxygen and nitrogen functional groups such as CN, O-C-N, O-C=O, N-C=O at edges whose strong electronegativity easily urges themselves negatively charged, but they would be affected by protonation or alkalization.

Meanwhile, FL intensity of 1-GQDs also drops a lot (Fig. 3c) at high or low pH value, but which can be surprisingly recovered after neutralization for 4-GQDs, whereas cannot for 6-GQDs. Furthermore, the different exfoliation yields of GNs are obtained, 91.6% for 4-GQDs, 89.8% for 5-GQDs and only 76.9% for 6-GQDs (Fig. 3d), also meaning a significant fall of exfoliation effect by alkaline treatment.

In fact, both the colloidal stability and FL signal reflect the changes of surface functional groups. These negatively charged groups have the ability of stabilizing the positive charges well, so the acidic treatment (protonation) may only affect the charge distribution or balance on 1-GQDs surface to do them a temporary harm which can be recovered. However, the alkaline treatment (alkalization) probably alters the surface group structure to do them a permanent harm.

Subsequently, the structure and morphology of 6-GQDs are thoroughly analysed. The total atomic percent of oxygen and nitrogen atoms (O + N) on 6-GQDs (20.95%) is much lower than that on 1-GQDs (38.10%), with a decrease of nitrogen-containing groups from 13.97% to 2.78% and oxygen-containing groups from 24.13% to 18.17% in XPS (Fig. S15 in SI). In Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Fig. S16 in SI), the peaks of -NH2, -OH, -C=O disappear or are left a little, and from the C 1s fitting of XPS (Fig. S17 in SI), the oxygen and nitrogen groups prominently decrease too. The alkaline reduction of oxide has widely been proved [38] and the formation of free ammonia from the -NH2 groups possibly explains the phenomena.

Height distribution of 6-GQDs by AFM is 0.53–3.69 nm in Fig. 3e and Fig. S18 in SI, and the lateral size distribution by HR-TEM is 11.1–38.9 nm (26.1 nm in average) in Fig. 3f and Fig. S19 in SI. Compared with those of 1-GQDs, the apparent agglomeration of 6-GQDs is confirmed, which is attributed to the destruction of functional groups, and it is the reason of consequent drop of water stability and exfoliation yield of GNs. Therefore, the type and amount of functional groups on GQDs surface are proved to play a vital role in graphene preparation.

Comparably, we prepared 7-GQDs by chemical oxidation using strong oxidant (see the Experimental section). Their height distribution by AFM is 0.34–1.20 nm in Fig. 4a and b, and the lateral size distribution by HR-TEM is 3.0–14.0 nm (7.5 nm in average) in Fig. 4c and Fig. S20 in SI. Large numbers of basal plane defects are observed in Fig. 4d by HR-TEM, and the much higher percent of oxygen atoms (39.56%) by XPS is shown (Fig. S21 in SI), and the bigger interlayer spacing of 7-GQDs (0.365 nm) by XRD is obtained (Fig. S22 in SI), meaning a higher extent of oxidation and damage. Moreover, a high ratio (4.46:1) of total amount of functionalized sp 2 (F-sp 2, 284.3 eV) and sp 3 CC bond (F-sp 3, 285.2 eV) to un-functionalized sp 2 CC bond (NF-sp 2, 284.6 eV) by C 1s fitting (Fig. S23 and Table S4 in SI) is detected on 7-GQDs, much higher than that of 1-GQDs (1.06:1), also a confirmation of severely damaged basal plane structure, which gives a rather low yield of GNs (52.8%), indicative of an irreparable loss of damaged basal plane, no matter how many oxygen groups they have.

Exfoliation mechanism using GQDs as stabilizer and exfoliation agent can be proposed in Fig. 5 . As a raw material, the expanded graphite basically has weak interlayer forces and small thickness (~15 nm) [39], which facilitate the exfoliation in nature. Moreover, the amphiphilic structure of GQDs with hydrophobic basal plane and hydrophilic oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups at edge not only favors GQDs to adsorb onto the graphite surface through non-covalent π-π conjugation, but facilitates the graphite to disperse into the water through H-bonds. For well-dispersed expanded graphite sheets, the cavitation behavior of sonication produces a strong tensile stress [40,41], which enlarges the interlayer space and weakens the van der Waals force among graphite lattice. The detailed mechanism of LPE process is as follows: GQDs are firstly adsorbed onto the graphite surface by van der Waals force. Secondly, under a strong electrostatic attraction force of H-bonds between GQDs and water and an assistant mechanical action of sonication, GQDs can be desorbed and dispersed into water. Therefore, together with the GQDs, the graphite surface is peeled off and entered the aqueous solution. Finally, the new surface which is exposed after exfoliation undergoes re-adsorption and re-exfoliation until the graphite is completely dissociated. Analogous to the microcleavage exfoliation of highly crystalline mono-layer graphene known as “scotch-tape” method by Geim [1], this process can be visually depicted as a “scotch-tape-like exfoliation” by GQDs in water.

Thereby this physical exfoliation process may not introduce the structural defects on basal plane of GNs, which is a reason of outstanding electrical conductivity, and simultaneously endow GNs the long-term water stability via the electrostatic repulsion of adsorbed GQDs (a higher ζ of −61.90 mV for GNs than −33.50 mV for 1-GQDs). A combined exfoliation effect from the 3-GQDs and 2-GQDs can also be explained. The former (large size) provides a large basal plane area for firm adsorption of GQDs on graphite surface, while the latter (small size) has more flexibility to occupy the edge or void of graphite surface to provide functional groups (Fig. S24 in SI) for dispersing. Consequently, the combined effect between 3-GQDs and 2-GQDs as exfoliation agent and stabilizer is beneficial to achieving ~100% yield. As for 6-GQDs, whose surface groups are irreversibly removed after alkaline treatment, the hydrophilic ability and H-bond force are unavoidably weakened, resulting in an easy agglomeration and the decrease of exfoliation yield. For 7-GQDs, the weak π-π conjugation force (similar to the weak adhesion of scotch tape) between GQDs and graphite surface due to basal plane defects cannot effectively exfoliate the few-layer GNs, even though the strong electrostatic attraction between lots of hydrophilic groups with water.

In summary, we use amphiphilic GQDs as stabilizer and exfoliation agent of LPE to prepare the high-quality GNs in water. The lateral size, hydrophilic functional groups at edge and the completeness of basal plane of GQDs are three important structure factors for efficient exfoliation of graphene (even to ~100% yield). The preparation mechanism can be visually depicted as a “scotch-tape-like exfoliation” by GQDs in water. This physical exfoliation process may not introduce structural defects to GNs, guaranteeing their outstanding physical properties. Meanwhile, the adsorbed GQDs endow GNs a long-term colloidal stability in water, making them useful for practical application such as in electrical and electrochemical device.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2019.03.290.
